instruction: Fix the AndroidManifest.xml permission mistake. Return only the corrected minimal manifest snippet.
input:
Requirement:
A WebView app needs internet access. Add the correct Android INTERNET permission in the correct location.

Bad answer:
<application
    android:name="com.google.android.apps.webview"
    android:icon="@drawable/ic_webview"
    android:label="@string/app_name"
    android:permission="INTERNET" />

instruction: Fix the invalid launcher Activity manifest entry. Return only the corrected <application> snippet.
input:
Requirement:
Declare .MainActivity inside <application> with MAIN and LAUNCHER intent-filter.

Bad answer:
<application>
    <activity>
        <activity-name>MainActivity</activity-name>
        <activity-version>1.0</activity-version>
        <intent-filter>
            <action>com.example.android.MainActivity</action>
            <category>com.example.android.MainActivity</category>
        </intent-filter>
    </activity>
</application>

instruction: Replace the bad Android storage code with minimal Java code that opens ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT_TREE and persists the returned Uri permission. Return code only.
input:
Requirement:
Launch Android's folder picker with ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT_TREE and persist the Uri permission in onActivityResult.

Bad pattern:
- imports android.provider.MediaStore.Files
- uses Environment or raw file paths
- does not call takePersistableUriPermission
- does not use Intent.ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT_TREE

instruction: Fix the bad file-listing approach. Use DocumentFile for a persisted SAF tree Uri. Avoid java.io.File, MediaStore, databases, and raw paths. Return code only.
input:
Requirement:
Given a persisted tree Uri, recursively collect file names under that folder using DocumentFile.

Bad answer:
Uses java.io.File, ContentResolver, SQLiteDatabase, MediaStore.Files, or returns null.

instruction: Fix the WebView Activity to be programmatic and complete. It must enable JavaScript, set a WebViewClient, load https://example.com, and include the INTERNET permission. Return the corrected answer only.
Fix the Kotlin Activity. Return one valid Kotlin MainActivity file that creates a TextView programmatically and uses it as the content view. Do not use XML.
Fix the Activity lifecycle explanation. Keep it under 120 words and mention onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, and onDestroy.
input:
Bad explanation:
onCreate, onStart, and onResume all start the main thread. onPause, onStop, and onDestroy stop the main thread. These methods display a title, start tasks, or show progress bars.

Problems:
- inaccurate main-thread claims
- repetitive
- does not explain visibility/interaction differences
output: An Activity is one screen. onCreate runs when it is first created, so set up views and saved state there. onStart runs when the screen becomes visible. onResume runs when the user can interact with it. onPause runs when another screen partly covers it; pause quick work there. onStop runs when it is no longer visible. onDestroy runs when the Activity is finishing or being removed. Use these callbacks to start, pause, save, and clean up work at the right time.

instruction: Fix the explanation of minSdk, targetSdk, and compileSdk. Keep it concise and correct.
input:
Bad explanation:
minSdk is the minimum SDK required to build your project. targetSdk is the maximum SDK you want to use. compileSdk is the minimum SDK used for the build.

Problems:
- minSdk controls install/device compatibility, not build
- targetSdk is not a maximum SDK
- compileSdk controls compile-time API access, not install compatibility
output: minSdk is the oldest Android version your app supports and can install on. targetSdk tells Android which platform behavior your app is designed for; newer target values may enable newer compatibility rules. compileSdk is the Android SDK version used to compile the app, so it controls which APIs your code can reference at build time. Device support is mainly controlled by minSdk, not compileSdk.
